instruction: Explain the CAPS assessment requirements for None (grades-r-3 phase).
output:
ASSESSMENT 
4.1 
Introduction 
Assessment is a continuous, planned process of identifying, gathering and interpreting information about the 
performance of learners using various methods of assessment. This involves four steps: generating and collecting 
evidence of achievement; evaluating this evidence; recording the findings and using this information to understand 
and thereby assisting the learner’s development in order to improve the process of learning and teaching. 
Assessment involves activities that are undertaken incrementally throughout the year. In Grades 10 - 12 assessment 
should be both informal (Assessment for Learning) and formal (Assessment of Learning). In both cases regular 
feedback should be provided to learners to inform the continuous learning experience. 
 
4.2 
Informal and Daily Assessment 
Assessment for learning has the purpose of continuously collecting information on a learner’s achievement to be 
used to inform and improve their learning. 
Informal assessment is the daily monitoring of learners’ progress. Examples of Informal Assessment are: 
observations, discussions, debates, practical demonstrations, learner-educator conferences, informal classroom 
interactions, questions and answers, short written activities completed during the learning process such as writing 
in-role, creative writing, informal classroom performances, case studies, etc. Informal assessment may be as simple 
as stopping during the learning process to observe learners or to discuss with learners how learning is progressing. 
Informal assessment can be used to develop learners’ knowledge, skills and values, assess learners’ strengths and 
weaknesses, provide additional support to learners, revisit or revise certain sections of the curriculum and motivate 
and encourage learners. It should also be used to inform planning for teaching and learning, but need not be 
recorded. It should not be seen as separate from learning activities taking place in the classroom. Learners or 
educators may mark these Assessment Tasks. 
Self-assessment and peer assessment actively involve learners in the assessment. This is important as it allows 
learners to learn from and reflect on their own performance. The results of the informal daily assessment tasks are 
not formally recorded unless the educator wishes to do so. In such instances, a simple checklist may be used to 
record this assessment. However, educators may use the learners’ performance in these assessment tasks to 
provide verbal or written feedback to learners, the School Management Team and parents. This is particularly 
important if barriers to learning or poor levels of participation are encountered. 
The results of daily Assessment Tasks are not taken into account for promotion and certification purposes. 
4.3 
Assessment 
All Assessment Tasks that make up a Formal Programme of Assessment for the year are regarded as Formal 
Assessment. Formal Assessment Tasks are marked and formally recorded an

instruction: Explain the CAPS assessment requirements for None (grades-r-3 phase).
output:
4.1. FORMAL ASSESSMENT 
 
 
All assessment tasks that make up a formal programme of assessment for the year are regarded as Formal Assessment. Formal 
assessment tasks are marked and formally recorded by the teacher for progression and certification purposes. All Formal 
Assessment tasks are subject to moderation for the purpose of quality assurance and to ensure that appropriate standards are 
maintained. 
 
Formal assessment provides teachers with a systematic way of evaluating how well learners are progressing in a grade and in 
a subject. Examples of formal assessments include tests, examinations, practical tasks, projects, oral presentations, 
demonstrations, performances, etc. Formal assessment tasks form part of a year-long formal Programme of Assessment in 
each grade and subject. 
 
4.1.1. Control tests & examinations in Grade 12 
 
 
Control tests and examinations are written under controlled conditions within a specified period of time. Control Tests should be 
comprised of ONE paper and Examinations should comprise of TWO papers. Control tests should cover the content for the 
whole term as well as content from grades 10 and 11 that is examinable in grade 12, as given in Table 6. Questions in tests and 
examinations should assess performance at different cognitive levels with an emphasis on process skills, critical thinking, 
scientific reasoning and strategies to investigate and solve problems in a variety of scientific, technological, environmental and 
everyday contexts. Questions in tests and examinations should assess performance with an emphasis on innovative thinking that 
includes a variety of problem solving methodologies. 
 
Examination papers and control tests in Physical Sciences Grade 12 should adhere to the weighting of cognitive levels given in 
Table 1. See APPENDIX 1 in the Physical Sciences Curriculum Policy and Assessment Statement for a detailed description of 
the cognitive levels.

scenario. The findings of the survey will be presented to a target audience indicated in the 
instructions. This section of the task should be done under controlled conditions. At the end of the 
duration the questionnaire must be handed in/submitted for marking.

Present the questionnaire to respondents for completing. The responses must then be processed 
in a spreadsheet according to the instructions given. Findings of survey will be submitted together 
with the completed questionnaires. Section 2 to be handed in at a date stipulated in the instructions 
of the task.  
The survey can also be presented as an integrated task (practical and theory) in which some 
questions/instructions will assess the theory aspects of Information Management. Skills and 
knowledge assessed must accommodate the required cognitive levels. 
Case study 
Case studies are in-depth investigations of real-life situation. Data is gathered from a variety of 
sources and by using several different methods. A case study involves an in-depth and detailed 
examination of a scenario, as well as its related contextual conditions. 
The duration of a case study should be 45 to 60 minutes. 
4.3.1.4 Examinations 
Practical Assessment Task (25% of the total marks for the subject) 
The Practical Assessment Task assesses the learner’s procedural skills and individual interaction 
with data and information as well as the way in which he or she processes, manipulates and 
presents the information. The information will finally be presented in a number of documents. 
These must be presented in the four application programs studied.  
The PAT focuses on Information Management and the use of ICTs and application software. It 
covers the following: 
• 
Identifying, finding and accessing information/data 
• 
Using, manipulating and processing information and data gathered 
• 
Presentation of solutions/findings/recommendations 
Evidence of the process must be provided in a series of draft documents. 
Each task must include a declaration of authenticity.

45 
Paper 1: Practical paper (25% of the total marks for the subject) 
This will be a practically oriented paper covering questions on Solution Development. 
To successfully complete this paper, each learner must have access to his or her own computer in 
the exam room. Provision needs to be made for sufficient computers to enable the examination to 
be completed in two sittings. 
This paper assesses the practical skills pertaining to Solution Development, that is the application 
packages studied, namely word processing, spreadsheets and databases as well as creating a 
simple web page using HTML. Presentations will only be assessed in the Practical Assessment 
Task and NOT in the examinations. These skills will

OVERVIEW OF CONTENT AND SCOPE PER TOPIC 
3.1 Solution Development 
Solution development is the actions and processes involved in developing a computer-based 
solution by utilising appropriate tools such as application packages to solve a variety of problems 
represented by real-life scenarios. For Computer Applications Technology these applications are 
end-user applications which include word-processing, spreadsheet, database and presentation 
applications.

instruction: Explain the CAPS assessment requirements for None (grades-r-3 phase).
output:
ASSESSMENT IN COMPUTER APPLICATIONS TECHNOLOGY 
4.1 Introduction 
Assessment is a continuous planned process of identifying, gathering and interpreting information 
about the performance of learners, using various forms of assessment. It involves four steps: 
generating and collecting evidence of achievement; evaluating this evidence; recording the findings 
and using this information to understand and thereby assist the learner’s development to improve 
the process of learning and teaching.  
Assessment involves activities that are undertaken throughout the year. In grades 10 – 12 
assessment comprises 2 different but related activities: informal daily assessment (assessment for 
learning) and formal assessment (assessment of learning).  
Assessment in CAT should encourage computational thinking practices that are integrating the 
power of human thinking with the capabilities of ICTs and application packages.  
4.2 Informal or daily assessment  
Assessment for learning has the purpose of continuously collecting information on a learner’s 
achievement that can be used to improve their learning. 
Informal assessment is the daily monitoring of learners’ progress. This is done through observations, 
discussions, practical demonstrations, learner-teacher conferences, informal classroom interactions, 
etc. Informal assessment may be as simple as stopping during the lesson to observe learners or to 
discuss with learners how learning is progressing. Informal assessment should be used to provide 
feedback to the learners and to inform planning for teaching, but need not be recorded. It should not 
be seen as separate from learning activities taking place in the classroom. Learners or teachers can 
mark these assessment tasks. 
Self-assessment and peer assessment actively involves learners in assessment. This is important 
as it allows learners to learn from and reflect on their own performance. The results of the informal 
daily assessment tasks are not formally recorded unless the teacher wishes to do so. The results of 
daily assessment tasks are not used for promotion and certification purposes.  
4.3 Formal assessment  
All assessment tasks that make up a formal programme of assessment for the year are regarded as 
formal assessment. Formal assessment tasks are marked and formally recorded by the teacher for 
progression and certification purposes. All formal assessment tasks are subject to moderation for 
the purpose of quality assurance and to ensure that appropriate standards are maintained. 
Formal assessment provides teachers with a systematic way of evaluating how well learners are 
progressing in a grade and in a particular subject. Examples of formal assessments include tests, 
examinations, practical tasks, alternative task. Formal assessment tasks form part of a year-long 
formal programme of assessment in each grade and subject. 
The following tables provide the formal assessment requirements for Computer Applications 
Technology:

instruction: Give the content specific clarification and term-by-term breakdown for None (grades-r-3 phase).
output:
CONTENT AND SCOPE PER TOPIC 
3.1 Solution Development 
Solution development is the development of software in a planned and structured process and is 
based on solving computational problems which include data-related problems through logical 
thinking. It involves the practices of algorithm development and creating a software solution 
according to a set of rules and/or requirements specified in the problem statement or by a 
client/business/individual. The software is developed using appropriate problem-solving techniques, 
tools and methodologies. Software solution development is achieved through computer 
programming which could be based on a single or combination of development paradigms such as 
event-driven programming, object-oriented programming and sequential programming. 
Broad topic layout and progression 
 
Note: 
In Grade 10 learners are introduced to important computational skills and concepts, algorithm development, problem solving and 
programming using a high-level programming language that uses an integrated development environment with a GUI builder. 
Learners are introduced to controls and code and event handling principles. 
In Grade 11, learners build on the principles and concepts learned in Grade 10. Event handling principles are reinforced using the 
form class, attributes, methods and controls. Skills to manipulate a database through code constructs are also introduced in Grade 11. 
In Grade 12, the principles and constructs are further emphasised through more advanced concepts and problems. Learners are 
introduced to basic object-oriented programming (OOP) and should be ready to engage with basic structured query language (SQL) 
code and manipulating a relational database. 
The development of computational thinking practices of algorithm development, problem solving and programming underpin solution 
development and should be emphasised from Grade 10 to Grade 12. 
Usability, HCI (human computer interaction) and software engineering principles should be reinforced as part of software development.

Sub-topic layout and progression for Solution Development 
 
Note: 
Algorithmic problem solving in Grade 10 should be dealt with separately at first as an introduction to solution development to develop 
the learner’s computational thinking practices of algorithm development, problem solving and programming using everyday 
scenarios. 
Learners should develop an understanding of the importance of order and precision when developing an algorithm as well as the 
place of algorithms in software solutions and computing science. Thereafter it should be reinforced, extended and integrated with 
solution development and programming. 
Solution development includes computational thinking and the application of software engineering principles using event-driven 
programming within the object-oriented (OO) paradigm. 
Learners should be able to use appropriate practices and tools to: 
• solve computational problems through: 
▪ identifying and analysing requirements for a specific problem; 
▪ designing effective algorithms; 
▪ converting these to code; and 
▪ testing the solution to see if it meets the requirements. 
• apply the principles of human computer interaction to design functional user interfaces.

3.2 Communication Technologies 
Communication technologies include various network technologies to facilitate the management and 
dissemination of digital data from one point to another. Communication technologies also refer to the 
electronic systems used for electronic data interchange that facilitate, among others, communication 
and information dissemination between various individuals or groups at a single point or dispersed 
locations. 
Broad topic layout and progression 
 
Note: 
Communication Technologies should teach learners to: 
• understand the concepts of the various technologies, standards and protocols involved in the electronic transmission of data via a 
computer-based network; 
• understand the conc

instruction: Explain the CAPS assessment requirements for None (grades-4-6 phase).
output:
ASSESSMENT 
4.1 
Introduction 
Assessment is a continuous, planned process of identifying, gathering and interpreting information about the 
performance of learners using various methods of assessment. This involves four steps: generating and collecting 
evidence of achievement; evaluating this evidence; recording the findings and using this information to understand 
and thereby assisting the learner’s development in order to improve the process of learning and teaching. 
Assessment involves activities that are undertaken incrementally throughout the year. In Grades 10 - 12 assessment 
should be both informal (Assessment for Learning) and formal (Assessment of Learning). In both cases regular 
feedback should be provided to learners to inform the continuous learning experience. 
 
4.2 
Informal and Daily Assessment 
Assessment for learning has the purpose of continuously collecting information on a learner’s achievement to be 
used to inform and improve their learning. 
Informal assessment is the daily monitoring of learners’ progress. Examples of Informal Assessment are: 
observations, discussions, debates, practical demonstrations, learner-educator conferences, informal classroom 
interactions, questions and answers, short written activities completed during the learning process such as writing 
in-role, creative writing, informal classroom performances, case studies, etc. Informal assessment may be as simple 
as stopping during the learning process to observe learners or to discuss with learners how learning is progressing. 
Informal assessment can be used to develop learners’ knowledge, skills and values, assess learners’ strengths and 
weaknesses, provide additional support to learners, revisit or revise certain sections of the curriculum and motivate 
and encourage learners. It should also be used to inform planning for teaching and learning, but need not be 
recorded. It should not be seen as separate from learning activities taking place in the classroom. Learners or 
educators may mark these Assessment Tasks. 
Self-assessment and peer assessment actively involve learners in the assessment. This is important as it allows 
learners to learn from and reflect on their own performance. The results of the informal daily assessment tasks are 
not formally recorded unless the educator wishes to do so. In such instances, a simple checklist may be used to 
record this assessment. However, educators may use the learners’ performance in these assessment tasks to 
provide verbal or written feedback to learners, the School Management Team and parents. This is particularly 
important if barriers to learning or poor levels of participation are encountered. 
The results of daily Assessment Tasks are not taken into account for promotion and certification purposes. 
4.3 
Assessment 
All Assessment Tasks that make up a Formal Programme of Assessment for the year are regarded as Formal 
Assessment. Formal Assessment Tasks are marked and formally recorded an

instruction: Explain the CAPS assessment requirements for None (grades-4-6 phase).
output:
4.1. FORMAL ASSESSMENT 
 
 
All assessment tasks that make up a formal programme of assessment for the year are regarded as Formal Assessment. Formal 
assessment tasks are marked and formally recorded by the teacher for progression and certification purposes. All Formal 
Assessment tasks are subject to moderation for the purpose of quality assurance and to ensure that appropriate standards are 
maintained. 
 
Formal assessment provides teachers with a systematic way of evaluating how well learners are progressing in a grade and in 
a subject. Examples of formal assessments include tests, examinations, practical tasks, projects, oral presentations, 
demonstrations, performances, etc. Formal assessment tasks form part of a year-long formal Programme of Assessment in 
each grade and subject. 
 
4.1.1. Control tests & examinations in Grade 12 
 
 
Control tests and examinations are written under controlled conditions within a specified period of time. Control Tests should be 
comprised of ONE paper and Examinations should comprise of TWO papers. Control tests should cover the content for the 
whole term as well as content from grades 10 and 11 that is examinable in grade 12, as given in Table 6. Questions in tests and 
examinations should assess performance at different cognitive levels with an emphasis on process skills, critical thinking, 
scientific reasoning and strategies to investigate and solve problems in a variety of scientific, technological, environmental and 
everyday contexts. Questions in tests and examinations should assess performance with an emphasis on innovative thinking that 
includes a variety of problem solving methodologies. 
 
Examination papers and control tests in Physical Sciences Grade 12 should adhere to the weighting of cognitive levels given in 
Table 1. See APPENDIX 1 in the Physical Sciences Curriculum Policy and Assessment Statement for a detailed description of 
the cognitive levels.

in a spreadsheet according to the instructions given. Findings of survey will be submitted together

The survey can also be presented as an integrated task (practical and theory) in which some 
questions/instructions will assess the theory aspects of Information Management. Skills and 
knowledge assessed must accommodate the required cognitive levels. 
Case study 
Case studies are in-depth investigations of real-life situation. Data is gathered from a variety of 
sources and by using several different methods. A case study involves an in-depth and detailed 
examination of a scenario, as well as its related contextual conditions. 
The duration of a case study should be 45 to 60 minutes. 
4.3.1.4 Examinations 
Practical Assessment Task (25% of the total marks for the subject) 
The Practical Assessment Task assesses the learner’s procedural skills and individual interaction 
with data and information as well as the way in which he or she processes, manipulates and 
presents the information. The information will finally be presented in a number of documents. 
These must be presented in the four application programs studied.  
The PAT focuses on Information Management and the use of ICTs and application software. It 
covers the following: 
• 
Identifying, finding and accessing information/data 
• 
Using, manipulating and processing information and data gathered 
• 
Presentation of solutions/findings/recommendations 
Evidence of the process must be provided in a series of draft documents. 
Each task must include a declaration of authenticity.

be completed in two sittings.

instruction: Explain the CAPS assessment requirements for None (grades-4-6 phase).
output:
ASSESSMENT IN COMPUTER APPLICATIONS TECHNOLOGY 
4.1 Introduction 
Assessment is a continuous planned process of identifying, gathering and interpreting information 
about the performance of learners, using various forms of assessment. It involves four steps: 
generating and collecting evidence of achievement; evaluating this evidence; recording the findings 
and using this information to understand and thereby assist the learner’s development to improve 
the process of learning and teaching.  
Assessment involves activities that are undertaken throughout the year. In grades 10 – 12 
assessment comprises 2 different but related activities: informal daily assessment (assessment for 
learning) and formal assessment (assessment of learning).  
Assessment in CAT should encourage computational thinking practices that are integrating the 
power of human thinking with the capabilities of ICTs and application packages.  
4.2 Informal or daily assessment  
Assessment for learning has the purpose of continuously collecting information on a learner’s 
achievement that can be used to improve their learning. 
Informal assessment is the daily monitoring of learners’ progress. This is done through observations, 
discussions, practical demonstrations, learner-teacher conferences, informal classroom interactions, 
etc. Informal assessment may be as simple as stopping during the lesson to observe learners or to 
discuss with learners how learning is progressing. Informal assessment should be used to provide 
feedback to the learners and to inform planning for teaching, but need not be recorded. It should not 
be seen as separate from learning activities taking place in the classroom. Learners or teachers can 
mark these assessment tasks. 
Self-assessment and peer assessment actively involves learners in assessment. This is important 
as it allows learners to learn from and reflect on their own performance. The results of the informal 
daily assessment tasks are not formally recorded unless the teacher wishes to do so. The results of 
daily assessment tasks are not used for promotion and certification purposes.  
4.3 Formal assessment  
All assessment tasks that make up a formal programme of assessment for the year are regarded as 
formal assessment. Formal assessment tasks are marked and formally recorded by the teacher for 
progression and certification purposes. All formal assessment tasks are subject to moderation for 
the purpose of quality assurance and to ensure that appropriate standards are maintained. 
Formal assessment provides teachers with a systematic way of evaluating how well learners are 
progressing in a grade and in a particular subject. Examples of formal assessments include tests, 
examinations, practical tasks, alternative task. Formal assessment tasks form part of a year-long 
formal programme of assessment in each grade and subject. 
The following tables provide the formal assessment requirements for Computer Applications 
Technology:

instruction: Give the content specific clarification and term-by-term breakdown for None (grades-4-6 phase).
output:
CONTENT AND SCOPE PER TOPIC 
3.1 Solution Development 
Solution development is the development of software in a planned and structured process and is 
based on solving computational problems which include data-related problems through logical 
thinking. It involves the practices of algorithm development and creating a software solution 
according to a set of rules and/or requirements specified in the problem statement or by a 
client/business/individual. The software is developed using appropriate problem-solving techniques, 
tools and methodologies. Software solution development is achieved through computer 
programming which could be based on a single or combination of development paradigms such as 
event-driven programming, object-oriented programming and sequential programming. 
Broad topic layout and progression 
 
Note: 
In Grade 10 learners are introduced to important computational skills and concepts, algorithm development, problem solving and 
programming using a high-level programming language that uses an integrated development environment with a GUI builder. 
Learners are introduced to controls and code and event handling principles. 
In Grade 11, learners build on the principles and concepts learned in Grade 10. Event handling principles are reinforced using the 
form class, attributes, methods and controls. Skills to manipulate a database through code constructs are also introduced in Grade 11. 
In Grade 12, the principles and constructs are further emphasised through more advanced concepts and problems. Learners are 
introduced to basic object-oriented programming (OOP) and should be ready to engage with basic structured query language (SQL) 
code and manipulating a relational database. 
The development of computational thinking practices of algorithm development, problem solving and programming underpin solution 
development and should be emphasised from Grade 10 to Grade 12. 
Usability, HCI (human computer interaction) and software engineering principles should be reinforced as part of software development.

Sub-topic layout and progression for Solution Development 
 
Note: 
Algorithmic problem solving in Grade 10 should be dealt with separately at first as an introduction to solution development to develop 
the learner’s computational thinking practices of algorithm development, problem solving and programming using everyday 
scenarios. 
Learners should develop an understanding of the importance of order and precision when developing an algorithm as well as the 
place of algorithms in software solutions and computing science. Thereafter it should be reinforced, extended and integrated with 
solution development and programming. 
Solution development includes computational thinking and the application of software engineering principles using event-driven 
programming within the object-oriented (OO) paradigm. 
Learners should be able to use appropriate practices and tools to: 
• solve computational problems through: 
▪ identifying and analysing requirements for a specific problem; 
▪ designing effective algorithms; 
▪ converting these to code; and 
▪ testing the solution to see if it meets the requirements. 
• apply the principles of human computer interaction to design functional user interfaces.

3.2 Communication Technologies 
Communication technologies include various network technologies to facilitate the management and 
dissemination of digital data from one point to another. Communication technologies also refer to the 
electronic systems used for electronic data interchange that facilitate, among others, communication 
and information dissemination between various individuals or groups at a single point or dispersed 
locations. 
Broad topic layout and progression 
 
Note: 
Communication Technologies should teach learners to: 
• understand the concepts of the various technologies, standards and protocols involved in the electronic transmission of data via a 
computer-based network; 
• understand the conc

instruction: Explain the CAPS assessment requirements for None (grades-4-6 phase).
output:
ASSESSMENT IN INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY 
4.1 
Introduction 
Assessment is a continuous planned process of identifying, gathering and interpreting information 
about the performance of learners, using various forms of assessment. It involves four steps: 
generating and collecting evidence of achievement, evaluating this evidence, recording the findings 
and using this information to understand and thereby assist the learner’s development to improve 
the process of learning and teaching. 
Assessment involves activities that are undertaken throughout the year. In grades 10 – 12 
assessment comprises two different but related activities: informal daily assessment (assessment 
for learning) and formal assessment (assessment of learning). 
Assessment in IT should encourage computational thinking practices, i.e. integrating the power of 
human thinking with the capabilities of ICTs and computer programming. 
4.2 
Informal or daily assessment 
Assessment for learning has the purpose of continuously collecting information on a learner’s 
achievement that can be used to improve their learning. 
Informal assessment is the daily monitoring of learners’ progress. This is done through observation, 
discussion, practical demonstrations, learner-teacher conferences, informal classroom interactions, 
etc. Informal assessment may be as simple as stopping during the lesson to observe learners or to 
discuss with learners how learning is progressing. Informal assessment should be used to provide 
feedback to the learners and to inform planning for teaching but need not be recorded. It should not 
be seen as separate from learning activities taking place in the classroom. Learners or teachers can 
mark these assessment tasks. 
Self-assessment and peer assessment actively involves learners in assessment. This is important 
as it allows learners to learn from and reflect on their own performance. The results of the informal 
daily assessment tasks are not formally recorded unless the teacher wishes to do so. The results of 
daily assessment tasks are not used for promotion and certification purposes. 
4.3 
Formal assessment 
All assessment tasks that make up a formal programme of assessment for the year are regarded as 
formal assessment. Formal assessment tasks are marked and formally recorded by the teacher for 
progression and certification purposes. All formal assessment tasks are subject to moderation for 
the purpose of quality assurance and to ensure that appropriate standards are maintained. 
Formal assessment provides teachers with a systematic way of evaluating how well learners are 
progressing in a grade and in a particular subject. Examples of formal assessments include tests, 
examinations, practical tasks, projects, etc. Formal assessment tasks form part of a year-long formal 
programme of assessment in each grade and subject. 
The following tables provide the formal assessment requirements for Information Technology:

Grade 10 
Formal Assessment 
During th

instruction: Explain the CAPS assessment requirements for None (grades-7-9 phase).
output:
ASSESSMENT 
4.1 
Introduction 
Assessment is a continuous, planned process of identifying, gathering and interpreting information about the 
performance of learners using various methods of assessment. This involves four steps: generating and collecting 
evidence of achievement; evaluating this evidence; recording the findings and using this information to understand 
and thereby assisting the learner’s development in order to improve the process of learning and teaching. 
Assessment involves activities that are undertaken incrementally throughout the year. In Grades 10 - 12 assessment 
should be both informal (Assessment for Learning) and formal (Assessment of Learning). In both cases regular 
feedback should be provided to learners to inform the continuous learning experience. 
 
4.2 
Informal and Daily Assessment 
Assessment for learning has the purpose of continuously collecting information on a learner’s achievement to be 
used to inform and improve their learning. 
Informal assessment is the daily monitoring of learners’ progress. Examples of Informal Assessment are: 
observations, discussions, debates, practical demonstrations, learner-educator conferences, informal classroom 
interactions, questions and answers, short written activities completed during the learning process such as writing 
in-role, creative writing, informal classroom performances, case studies, etc. Informal assessment may be as simple 
as stopping during the learning process to observe learners or to discuss with learners how learning is progressing. 
Informal assessment can be used to develop learners’ knowledge, skills and values, assess learners’ strengths and 
weaknesses, provide additional support to learners, revisit or revise certain sections of the curriculum and motivate 
and encourage learners. It should also be used to inform planning for teaching and learning, but need not be 
recorded. It should not be seen as separate from learning activities taking place in the classroom. Learners or 
educators may mark these Assessment Tasks. 
Self-assessment and peer assessment actively involve learners in the assessment. This is important as it allows 
learners to learn from and reflect on their own performance. The results of the informal daily assessment tasks are 
not formally recorded unless the educator wishes to do so. In such instances, a simple checklist may be used to 
record this assessment. However, educators may use the learners’ performance in these assessment tasks to 
provide verbal or written feedback to learners, the School Management Team and parents. This is particularly 
important if barriers to learning or poor levels of participation are encountered. 
The results of daily Assessment Tasks are not taken into account for promotion and certification purposes. 
4.3 
Assessment 
All Assessment Tasks that make up a Formal Programme of Assessment for the year are regarded as Formal 
Assessment. Formal Assessment Tasks are marked and formally recorded an

instruction: Explain the CAPS assessment requirements for None (grades-7-9 phase).
output:
4.1. FORMAL ASSESSMENT 
 
 
All assessment tasks that make up a formal programme of assessment for the year are regarded as Formal Assessment. Formal 
assessment tasks are marked and formally recorded by the teacher for progression and certification purposes. All Formal 
Assessment tasks are subject to moderation for the purpose of quality assurance and to ensure that appropriate standards are 
maintained. 
 
Formal assessment provides teachers with a systematic way of evaluating how well learners are progressing in a grade and in 
a subject. Examples of formal assessments include tests, examinations, practical tasks, projects, oral presentations, 
demonstrations, performances, etc. Formal assessment tasks form part of a year-long formal Programme of Assessment in 
each grade and subject. 
 
4.1.1. Control tests & examinations in Grade 12 
 
 
Control tests and examinations are written under controlled conditions within a specified period of time. Control Tests should be 
comprised of ONE paper and Examinations should comprise of TWO papers. Control tests should cover the content for the 
whole term as well as content from grades 10 and 11 that is examinable in grade 12, as given in Table 6. Questions in tests and 
examinations should assess performance at different cognitive levels with an emphasis on process skills, critical thinking, 
scientific reasoning and strategies to investigate and solve problems in a variety of scientific, technological, environmental and 
everyday contexts. Questions in tests and examinations should assess performance with an emphasis on innovative thinking that 
includes a variety of problem solving methodologies. 
 
Examination papers and control tests in Physical Sciences Grade 12 should adhere to the weighting of cognitive levels given in 
Table 1. See APPENDIX 1 in the Physical Sciences Curriculum Policy and Assessment Statement for a detailed description of 
the cognitive levels.

Case study 
Case studies are in-depth investigations of real-life situation. Data is gathered from a variety of 
sources and by using several different methods. A case study involves an in-depth and detailed 
examination of a scenario, as well as its related contextual conditions. 
The duration of a case study should be 45 to 60 minutes. 
4.3.1.4 Examinations 
Practical Assessment Task (25% of the total marks for the subject) 
The Practical Assessment Task assesses the learner’s procedural skills and individual interaction 
with data and information as well as the way in which he or she processes, manipulates and 
presents the information. The information will finally be presented in a number of documents. 
These must be presented in the four application programs studied.  
The PAT focuses on Information Management and the use of ICTs and application software. It 
covers the following: 
• 
Identifying, finding and accessing information/data 
• 
Using, manipulating and processing information and data gathered 
• 
Presentation of solutions/findings/recommendations 
Evidence of the process must be provided in a series of draft documents. 
Each task must include a declaration of authenticity.

To successfully complete this paper, each learner must have access to his or her own computer in

be completed in two sittings. 
This paper assesses the practical skills pertaining to Solution Development, that is the application 
packages studied, namely word processing, spreadsheets and databases as well as creating a

ASSESSMENT IN COMPUTER APPLICATIONS TECHNOLOGY 
4.1 Introduction 
Assessment is a continuous planned process of identifying, gathering and interpreting information 
about the performance of learners, using various forms of assessment. It involves four steps: 
generating and collecting evidence of achievement; evaluating this evidence; recording the findings 
and using this information to understand and thereby assist the learner’s development to improve 
the process of learning and teaching.  
Assessment involves activities that are undertaken throughout the year. In grades 10 – 12 
assessment comprises 2 different but related activities: informal daily assessment (assessment for 
learning) and formal assessment (assessment of learning).  
Assessment in CAT should encourage computational thinking practices that are integrating the 
power of human thinking with the capabilities of ICTs and application packages.  
4.2 Informal or daily assessment  
Assessment for learning has the purpose of continuously collecting information on a learner’s 
achievement that can be used to improve their learning. 
Informal assessment is the daily monitoring of learners’ progress. This is done through observations, 
discussions, practical demonstrations, learner-teacher conferences, informal classroom interactions, 
etc. Informal assessment may be as simple as stopping during the lesson to observe learners or to 
discuss with learners how learning is progressing. Informal assessment should be used to provide 
feedback to the learners and to inform planning for teaching, but need not be recorded. It should not 
be seen as separate from learning activities taking place in the classroom. Learners or teachers can 
mark these assessment tasks. 
Self-assessment and peer assessment actively involves learners in assessment. This is important 
as it allows learners to learn from and reflect on their own performance. The results of the informal 
daily assessment tasks are not formally recorded unless the teacher wishes to do so. The results of 
daily assessment tasks are not used for promotion and certification purposes.  
4.3 Formal assessment  
All assessment tasks that make up a formal programme of assessment for the year are regarded as 
formal assessment. Formal assessment tasks are marked and formally recorded by the teacher for 
progression and certification purposes. All formal assessment tasks are subject to moderation for 
the purpose of quality assurance and to ensure that appropriate standards are maintained. 
Formal assessment provides teachers with a systematic way of evaluating how well learners are 
progressing in a grade and in a particular subject. Examples of formal assessments include tests, 
examinations, practical tasks, alternative task. Formal assessment tasks form part of a year-long

instruction: Give the content specific clarification and term-by-term breakdown for None (grades-7-9 phase).
output:
CONTENT AND SCOPE PER TOPIC 
3.1 Solution Development 
Solution development is the development of software in a planned and structured process and is 
based on solving computational problems which include data-related problems through logical 
thinking. It involves the practices of algorithm development and creating a software solution 
according to a set of rules and/or requirements specified in the problem statement or by a 
client/business/individual. The software is developed using appropriate problem-solving techniques, 
tools and methodologies. Software solution development is achieved through computer 
programming which could be based on a single or combination of development paradigms such as 
event-driven programming, object-oriented programming and sequential programming. 
Broad topic layout and progression 
 
Note: 
In Grade 10 learners are introduced to important computational skills and concepts, algorithm development, problem solving and 
programming using a high-level programming language that uses an integrated development environment with a GUI builder. 
Learners are introduced to controls and code and event handling principles. 
In Grade 11, learners build on the principles and concepts learned in Grade 10. Event handling principles are reinforced using the 
form class, attributes, methods and controls. Skills to manipulate a database through code constructs are also introduced in Grade 11. 
In Grade 12, the principles and constructs are further emphasised through more advanced concepts and problems. Learners are 
introduced to basic object-oriented programming (OOP) and should be ready to engage with basic structured query language (SQL) 
code and manipulating a relational database. 
The development of computational thinking practices of algorithm development, problem solving and programming underpin solution 
development and should be emphasised from Grade 10 to Grade 12. 
Usability, HCI (human computer interaction) and software engineering principles should be reinforced as part of software development.

Sub-topic layout and progression for Solution Development 
 
Note: 
Algorithmic problem solving in Grade 10 should be dealt with separately at first as an introduction to solution development to develop 
the learner’s computational thinking practices of algorithm development, problem solving and programming using everyday 
scenarios. 
Learners should develop an understanding of the importance of order and precision when developing an algorithm as well as the 
place of algorithms in software solutions and computing science. Thereafter it should be reinforced, extended and integrated with 
solution development and programming. 
Solution development includes computational thinking and the application of software engineering principles using event-driven 
programming within the object-oriented (OO) paradigm. 
Learners should be able to use appropriate practices and tools to: 
• solve computational problems through: 
▪ identifying and analysing requirements for a specific problem; 
▪ designing effective algorithms; 
▪ converting these to code; and 
▪ testing the solution to see if it meets the requirements. 
• apply the principles of human computer interaction to design functional user interfaces.

3.2 Communication Technologies 
Communication technologies include various network technologies to facilitate the management and 
dissemination of digital data from one point to another. Communication technologies also refer to the 
electronic systems used for electronic data interchange that facilitate, among others, communication 
and information dissemination between various individuals or groups at a single point or dispersed 
locations. 
Broad topic layout and progression 
 
Note: 
Communication Technologies should teach learners to: 
• understand the concepts of the various technologies, standards and protocols involved in the electronic transmission of data via a 
computer-based network; 
• understand the conc

4.1 
Introduction 
Assessment is a continuous planned process of identifying, gathering and interpreting information 
about the performance of learners, using various forms of assessment. It involves four steps: 
generating and collecting evidence of achievement, evaluating this evidence, recording the findings 
and using this information to understand and thereby assist the learner’s development to improve 
the process of learning and teaching. 
Assessment involves activities that are undertaken throughout the year. In grades 10 – 12 
assessment comprises two different but related activities: informal daily assessment (assessment 
for learning) and formal assessment (assessment of learning). 
Assessment in IT should encourage computational thinking practices, i.e. integrating the power of 
human thinking with the capabilities of ICTs and computer programming. 
4.2 
Informal or daily assessment 
Assessment for learning has the purpose of continuously collecting information on a learner’s 
achievement that can be used to improve their learning. 
Informal assessment is the daily monitoring of learners’ progress. This is done through observation, 
discussion, practical demonstrations, learner-teacher conferences, informal classroom interactions, 
etc. Informal assessment may be as simple as stopping during the lesson to observe learners or to 
discuss with learners how learning is progressing. Informal assessment should be used to provide 
feedback to the learners and to inform planning for teaching but need not be recorded. It should not 
be seen as separate from learning activities taking place in the classroom. Learners or teachers can 
mark these assessment tasks. 
Self-assessment and peer assessment actively involves learners in assessment. This is important 
as it allows learners to learn from and reflect on their own performance. The results of the informal 
daily assessment tasks are not formally recorded unless the teacher wishes to do so. The results of 
daily assessment tasks are not used for promotion and certification purposes. 
4.3 
Formal assessment 
All assessment tasks that make up a formal programme of assessment for the year are regarded as 
formal assessment. Formal assessment tasks are marked and formally recorded by the teacher for 
progression and certification purposes. All formal assessment tasks are subject to moderation for 
the purpose of quality assurance and to ensure that appropriate standards are maintained. 
Formal assessment provides teachers with a systematic way of evaluating how well learners are 
progressing in a grade and in a particular subject. Examples of formal assessments include tests, 
examinations, practical tasks, projects, etc. Formal assessment tasks form part of a year-long formal 
programme of assessment in each grade and subject. 
The following tables provide the formal assessment requirements for Information Technology:

Grade 10 
Formal Assessment

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 2 Afrikaans.
output:
# Grade 2 Afrikaans First Additional Language (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: Groete en basiese gesprekke
- Week 2: Woordeskat (familie)
- Week 3: Lees eenvoudige sinne
- Week 4: Skryf kort sinne
- Week 5: Luister en praat (stories)
- Week 6: Grammatika (selfstandige naamwoorde)
- Week 7: Gedigte en liedjies
- Week 8: Hersiening & assessering

## Term 2
- Week 1: Woordeskat (skoolvoorwerpe)
- Week 2: Leesbegrip (kort tekste)
- Week 3: Skryf (paragrawe)
- Week 4: Luister en praat (dialoë)
- Week 5: Grammatika (werkwoorde)
- Week 6: Woordeskat (diere)
- Week 7: Mondelinge voordrag
- Week 8: Hersiening & assessering

## Term 3
- Week 1: Woordeskat (kos)
- Week 2: Leesbegrip (stories)
- Week 3: Skryf (briewe)
- Week 4: Luister en praat (instruksies)
- Week 5: Grammatika (byvoeglike naamwoorde)
- Week 6: Woordeskat (weer)
- Week 7: Mondelinge aanbiedings
- Week 8: Hersiening & assessering

## Term 4
- Week 1: Woordeskat (vervoer)
- Week 2: Leesbegrip (inligtingstekste)
- Week 3: Skryf (verslae)
- Week 4: Luister en praat (debatte)
- Week 5: Grammatika (leestekens)
- Week 6: Woordeskat (kultuur)
- Week 7: Mondelinge storievertelling
- Week 8: Hersiening & finale assessering

## Curriculum Resources
- [CAPS Document: home-languages-afrikaans.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade2/home-languages-afrikaans.pdf)
- [CAPS Document: life-skills-afrikaans.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade2/life-skills-afrikaans.pdf)
- [CAPS Document: mathematics-afrikaans.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade2/mathematics-afrikaans.pdf)

# Grade 2 Sepedi First Additional Language (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: Dumedišo le boitsebišo
- Week 2: Polelo ya malapa
- Week 3: Go bala mantšu a bonolo
- Week 4: Go ngwala dipolelo tše khutšo
- Week 5: Go theeletša le go bolela (dikanegelo)
- Week 6: Sebopego sa mantšu (maina)
- Week 7: Dithoko le dipina
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko & tekolo

## Term 2
- Week 1: Polelo ya dilo tša sekolo
- Week 2: Go bala ditemana tše khutšo
- Week 3: Go ngwala (diparagarafa)
- Week 4: Go theeletša le go bolela (dipoledi)
- Week 5: Sebopego sa mantšu (mašito)
- Week 6: Polelo ya diphoofolo
- Week 7: Dithoko tša molomo
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko & tekolo

## Term 3
- Week 1: Polelo ya dijo
- Week 2: Go bala dikanegelo
- Week 3: Go ngwala (makwalo)
- Week 4: Go theeletša le go bolela (ditaelo)
- Week 5: Sebopego sa mantšu (mahlatse)
- Week 6: Polelo ya leratadima
- Week 7: Dipontšho tša molomo
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko & tekolo

## Term 4
- Week 1: Polelo ya dipalangwa
- Week 2: Go bala ditemana tša tsebo
- Week 3: Go ngwala (dintlha)
- Week 4: Go theeletša le go bolela (dikgokagano)
- Week 5: Sebopego sa mantšu (dipontšo)
- Week 6: Polelo ya setšo
- Week 7: Dikanegelo tša molomo
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko & tekolo ya mafelelo

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 2 Arts culture.
output:
# Grade 2 Arts & Culture (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: Introduction to rhythm
- Week 2: Singing simple songs
- Week 3: Drawing shapes & patterns
- Week 4: Acting short skits
- Week 5: Dance basics
- Week 6: Colour mixing
- Week 7: Storytelling through drama
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 2
- Week 1: Percussion instruments
- Week 2: Singing in groups
- Week 3: Painting with primary colours
- Week 4: Mime & role play
- Week 5: Dance sequences
- Week 6: Collage making
- Week 7: Poetry performance
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 3
- Week 1: Music notation basics
- Week 2: Singing rounds
- Week 3: Drawing landscapes
- Week 4: Improvisation in drama
- Week 5: Dance choreography
- Week 6: Sculpture basics
- Week 7: Storytelling with puppets
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 4
- Week 1: Music composition basics
- Week 2: Singing folk songs
- Week 3: Painting portraits
- Week 4: Drama production
- Week 5: Dance performance
- Week 6: Mixed media art
- Week 7: Cultural showcase
- Week 8: Revision & final assessment

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 2 Life skills.
output:
# Grade 2 Life Skills (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: Myself & my family
- Week 2: Safety at home
- Week 3: Healthy eating
- Week 4: Physical education (basic movement)
- Week 5: Creative arts (drawing)
- Week 6: Environment awareness
- Week 7: Music & rhythm
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 2
- Week 1: My community
- Week 2: Safety at school
- Week 3: Hygiene practices
- Week 4: Physical education (games)
- Week 5: Creative arts (painting)
- Week 6: Recycling
- Week 7: Music & singing
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 3
- Week 1: My country
- Week 2: Road safety
- Week 3: Healthy lifestyle
- Week 4: Physical education (teamwork)
- Week 5: Creative arts (crafts)
- Week 6: Environmental care
- Week 7: Music & instruments
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 4
- Week 1: World cultures
- Week 2: Safety outdoors
- Week 3: Emotional well-being
- Week 4: Physical education (sports)
- Week 5: Creative arts (performance)
- Week 6: Environmental projects
- Week 7: Music & dance
- Week 8: Revision & final assessment

# Grade 2 Social Sciences (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: My family history
- Week 2: Local community history

- Week 4: Weather & seasons
- Week 5: Natural resources
- Week 6: Population basics
- Week 7: Transport systems
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

- Week 2: South African heritage
- Week 3: Geography of my province
- Week 4: Climate zones
- Week 5: Water resources
- Week 6: Settlement types
- Week 7: Communication systems
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

- Week 5: Minerals & mining
- Week 6: Population growth
- Week 7: Transport & trade
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 4

- Week 2: South African democracy
- Week 3: Geography of Africa
- Week 4: Climate change
- Week 5: Resource management
- Week 6: Urban vs rural settlements
- Week 7: Global communication
- Week 8: Revision & final assessment

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 2 Maths.
output:
# Grade 2 Mathematics (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: Counting to 200
- Week 2: Place value (hundreds, tens, ones)
- Week 3: Addition/subtraction within 100
- Week 4: Multiplication (2, 5, 10 tables)
- Week 5: Division (sharing, grouping)
- Week 6: Fractions (halves, quarters, thirds)
- Week 7: Geometry (2D shapes, symmetry)
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 2
- Week 1: Counting backwards
- Week 2: Expanded notation
- Week 3: Addition/subtraction with regrouping
- Week 4: Multiplication (3, 4 tables)
- Week 5: Division with remainders
- Week 6: Measurement (length, mass, capacity)
- Week 7: Data handling (bar graphs)
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 3
- Week 1: Counting to 500
- Week 2: Place value (thousands)
- Week 3: Fractions (fifths, tenths)
- Week 4: Geometry (3D shapes)
- Week 5: Measurement (time: hours, minutes)
- Week 6: Money (rand & cents)
- Week 7: Probability (likely/unlikely events)
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 4
- Week 1: Counting to 1000
- Week 2: Addition/subtraction with thousands
- Week 3: Multiplication (6, 7 tables)
- Week 4: Division (long division basics)
- Week 5: Geometry (angles, triangles)
- Week 6: Measurement (temperature)
- Week 7: Data handling (pie charts)
- Week 8: Revision & final assessment

## Curriculum Resources
- [CAPS Document: mathematics-setswana.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade2/mathematics-setswana.pdf)
- [CAPS Document: mathematics-xitsonga.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade2/mathematics-xitsonga.pdf)
- [CAPS Document: mathematics-isizulu.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade2/mathematics-isizulu.pdf)
- [CAPS Document: mathematics-english.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade2/mathematics-english.pdf)
- [CAPS Document: mathematics-sesotho.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade2/mathematics-sesotho.pdf)

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 2 English.
output:
# Grade 2 English Home Language (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: Phonics (consonant blends)
- Week 2: Reading comprehension (short passages)
- Week 3: Listening & speaking (dialogues)
- Week 4: Writing (paragraphs)
- Week 5: Grammar (verbs)
- Week 6: Vocabulary expansion
- Week 7: Creative writing (poems)
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 2
- Week 1: Phonics (long vowels)
- Week 2: Reading comprehension (stories)
- Week 3: Listening & speaking (debates)
- Week 4: Writing (letters)
- Week 5: Grammar (adjectives)
- Week 6: Vocabulary (synonyms/antonyms)
- Week 7: Creative writing (descriptive)
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 3
- Week 1: Phonics (complex sounds)
- Week 2: Reading comprehension (non-fiction)
- Week 3: Listening & speaking (presentations)
- Week 4: Writing (reports)
- Week 5: Grammar (punctuation)
- Week 6: Vocabulary (idioms)
- Week 7: Creative writing (narrative)
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 4
- Week 1: Literature (short stories)
- Week 2: Reading comprehension (poetry)
- Week 3: Listening & speaking (oral presentations)
- Week 4: Writing (essays)
- Week 5: Grammar (complex sentences)
- Week 6: Vocabulary (figurative language)
- Week 7: Creative writing (argumentative)
- Week 8: Revision & final assessment

## Curriculum Resources
- [CAPS Document: home-languages-afrikaans.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade2/home-languages-afrikaans.pdf)

# Grade 2 Natural Sciences (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: Living & non-living things
- Week 2: Plants & their parts
- Week 3: Animals & habitats
- Week 4: Weather patterns
- Week 5: Water cycle basics
- Week 6: Energy sources
- Week 7: Simple machines
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 2

- Week 2: Senses

- Week 4: Matter (solid, liquid, gas)
- Week 5: Heat & temperature
- Week 6: Light & shadows
- Week 7: Electricity basics
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 3

- Week 3: Ecosystems
- Week 4: Pollution
- Week 5: Recycling
- Week 6: Renewable energy
- Week 7: Space (sun, moon, stars)
- Week 8: Revision & assessment

## Term 4
- Week 1: Human health
- Week 2: Diseases & prevention
- Week 3: Weather & climate
- Week 4: Natural disasters
- Week 5: Conservation
- Week 6: Technology in everyday life
- Week 7: Scientific experiments
- Week 8: Revision & final assessment

# Graad 5 Afrikaans Eerste Addisionele Taal (KABV)

## Kwartaal 1
- Week 1: Luister en praat (Groete en nuus)
- Week 2: Lees en kyk (Kort stories)
- Week 3: Skryf en ontwerp (Basiese sinne)
- Week 4: Taalstrukture (Selfstandige naamwoorde)
- Week 5: My familie en vriende
- Week 6: Diere en hul maniere
- Week 7: Klanke en spelling
- Week 8: Assessering

## Kwartaal 2
- Week 1: My skool en klaskamer
- Week 2: Lees van feitelike tekste
- Week 3: Skryf van aanwysings
- Week 4: Taalstrukture (Werkwoorde)
- Week 5: Kos en lekkergoed
- Week 6: Seisoene en aktiwiteite
- Week 7: Mondelinge voordrag
- Week 8: Assessering

## Kwartaal 3
- Week 1: Gesondheid en veiligheid
- Week 2: Lees van gedigte
- Week 3: Skryf van kort briewe
- Week 4: Taalstrukture (Byvoeglike naamwoorde)
- Week 5: Vervoer en reis

- Week 8: Assessering

## Kwartaal 4

- Week 8: Finale Assessering

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 5 Sepedi.
output:
# Grade 5 Sepedi First Additional Language (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: Madume le polelo ya leago (Social language and greetings)
- Week 2: Go bala dikanegelo tše kopana (Short stories)
- Week 3: Go ngwala ditemana (Paragraph writing)
- Week 4: Sebopego sa polelo (Grammar: Maina le Madiri)
- Week 5: Polelo ya mmele (Body parts and health)
- Week 6: Meloko le ditswala (Family and relations)
- Week 7: Go theeletša direto (Listening to praises)
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko (Assessment)

## Term 2
- Week 1: Tikologo ya rena (Our environment)
- Week 2: Go bala dikanegelo tša go ruta (Fables)
- Week 3: Go ngwala ditaelo (Instructional writing)
- Week 4: Sebopego sa polelo (Grammar: Mašito)
- Week 5: Dipalangwa le maeto (Transport and travel)
- Week 6: Boemo bja boso (Weather and climate)
- Week 7: Go bolela (Debates)
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko (Assessment)

## Term 3
- Week 1: Dijo le matlotlo a tlhago (Food and natural resources)
- Week 2: Go bala ditaba (Reading news)
- Week 3: Go ngwala mangwalo (Letter writing)
- Week 4: Sebopego sa polelo (Grammar: Mahlatse)
- Week 5: Dipapadi le boitšhidullo (Sports and exercise)
- Week 6: Dišupatsela (Directions)
- Week 7: Go theeletša (Traditional stories)
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko (Assessment)

## Term 4
- Week 1: Setšo le bophelo (Culture and life)
- Week 2: Go bala dipoelo tša tsebo (Informational texts)
- Week 3: Go ngwala direto (Poetry writing)
- Week 4: Sebopego sa polelo (Revision)
- Week 5: Boitshwaro bja batho (Human behavior)
- Week 6: Go bolela ka molomo (Oral presentations)
- Week 7: Boitokišetšo bja ditlhahlobo (Exam prep)
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko ya mafelelo (Final assessment)

## Curriculum Resources
- [CAPS Document: home-languages-sepedi.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade5/home-languages-sepedi.pdf)

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 5 Arts culture.
output:
# Grade 5 Arts & Culture (CAPS)

## Term 1: Visual Arts & Music
- Week 1: Visual Arts (Colour and texture)
- Week 2: Visual Arts (Pattern and balance)
- Week 3: Visual Arts (Drawing: Human figures)
- Week 4: Music (Rhythm: Notation and beat)
- Week 5: Music (Pitch: High and low sounds)
- Week 6: Music (Musical forms: Rounds and Canons)
- Week 7: Integrating Art and Music
- Week 8: Assessment

## Term 2: Drama & Dance
- Week 1: Drama (Vocal modulation and projection)
- Week 2: Drama (Physical expression and mime)
- Week 3: Drama (Role play: Conflict and resolution)
- Week 4: Dance (Warm-up and flexibility)
- Week 5: Dance (Rhythmic movement and patterns)
- Week 6: Dance (Traditional dances of Southern Africa)
- Week 7: Choreographing a group dance
- Week 8: Assessment

## Term 3: Visual Arts & Drama
- Week 1: Visual Arts (3D Construction: Puppets)
- Week 2: Visual Arts (Mask making)
- Week 3: Visual Arts (Painting: Landscapes)
- Week 4: Drama (Puppetry and characterization)
- Week 5: Drama (Improvisation skills)
- Week 6: Drama (Script writing basics)
- Week 7: Integrating Art and Drama
- Week 8: Assessment

## Term 4: Music & Dance
- Week 1: Music (Musical instruments: String and Keyboards)
- Week 2: Music (Listening to diverse music genres)
- Week 3: Music (Final composition project)
- Week 4: Dance (Contemporary and modern steps)
- Week 5: Dance (Using space and dynamics)
- Week 6: Dance (Final performance rehearsals)
- Week 7: Final Arts Showcase
- Week 8: Final Assessment

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 5 Life skills.
output:
# Grade 5 Life Skills (CAPS)

## Term 1: Personal and Social Well-being (PSW)
- Week 1: Positive self-concept (Strengths and weaknesses)
- Week 2: Dealing with success and failure
- Week 3: Peer pressure (How to say no)
- Week 4: Child abuse (Safety and reporting)
- Week 5: Physical Education (Basic fitness and games)
- Week 6: Creative Arts (Drama: Storytelling)
- Week 7: Creative Arts (Visual Arts: Painting)
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 2: Health and Environment
- Week 1: Nutrition (Balanced diet and vitamins)
- Week 2: Environmental health (Safe water and food)
- Week 3: Communicable diseases (Prevention)
- Week 4: Safety in and around water
- Week 5: Physical Education (Rhythmic movements)
- Week 6: Creative Arts (Music: Instrumental basics)
- Week 7: Creative Arts (Visual Arts: Drawing)
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 3: Rights and Responsibilities
- Week 1: National symbols of South Africa
- Week 2: Religious festivals and customs
- Week 3: Cultural diversity and tolerance
- Week 4: Rights and responsibilities (Bill of Rights)
- Week 5: Physical Education (Athletics and sportsmanship)
- Week 6: Creative Arts (Drama: Role play)
- Week 7: Creative Arts (Visual Arts: Sculpture)
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 4: Careers and Safety
- Week 1: Knowledge of the world of work (Careers)
- Week 2: Traffic safety (Passenger and pedestrian)
- Week 3: Substance abuse (Tobacco and alcohol)
- Week 4: Physical Education (Swimming/Field games)
- Week 5: Creative Arts (Music: Traditional songs)
- Week 6: Creative Arts (Visual Arts: Design)
- Week 7: Final Revision
- Week 8: Final Assessment

## Curriculum Resources
- [CAPS Document: life-skills.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade5/life-skills.pdf)

- Week 1: Positive self-concept (Strengths and weaknesses)
- Week 2: Dealing with success and failure
- Week 3: Peer pressure (How to say no)
- Week 4: Child abuse (Safety and reporting)
- Week 5: Physical Education (Basic fitness and games)
- Week 6: Creative Arts (Drama: Storytelling)
- Week 7: Creative Arts (Visual Arts: Painting)
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 5 Social sciences.
output:
# Grade 5 Social Sciences (CAPS)

## Term 1: Geography - Map skills
- Week 1: World maps and globes
- Week 2: Latitude and longitude (Basics)
- Week 3: Compass directions (8 points)
- Week 4: Map scales
- Week 5: Physical maps of Africa
- Week 6: Physical maps of South Africa
- Week 7: Symbols and keys (Advanced)
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 2: History - Hunter-gatherers and herders
- Week 1: Introduction to history (Sources)
- Week 2: The San (Hunter-gatherers)
- Week 3: San social organization and beliefs
- Week 4: San rock art
- Week 5: The Khoikhoi (Herders)
- Week 6: Interaction between San and Khoikhoi
- Week 7: Archaeology and historical evidence
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 3: Geography - Physical features of South Africa
- Week 1: Mountains and rivers of SA
- Week 2: Climate and vegetation zones
- Week 3: Settlement patterns and environment
- Week 4: Mining and physical features
- Week 5: Water in South Africa (Dams and rivers)
- Week 6: Weather patterns and data
- Week 7: Map of SA provinces and cities
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 4: History - An ancient African society: Mapungubwe
- Week 1: Farmers in South Africa (First farmers)
- Week 2: Social organization of early farmers
- Week 3: The Kingdom of Mapungubwe
- Week 4: Trade and contact with the outside world
- Week 5: Significance of Mapungubwe (The Golden Rhino)
- Week 6: Great Zimbabwe (Brief intro)
- Week 7: Final Revision
- Week 8: Final Assessment

## Curriculum Resources
- [CAPS Document: social-sciences.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade5/social-sciences.pdf)

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 5 Maths.
output:
# Grade 5 Mathematics (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: Whole numbers (Ordering and comparing up to 6 digits)
- Week 2: Whole numbers (Addition and subtraction)
- Week 3: Numeric patterns (Flow diagrams)
- Week 4: Whole numbers (Multiplication by 2-digit numbers)
- Week 5: Whole numbers (Division by 2-digit numbers)
- Week 6: Time (Calculations and conversions)
- Week 7: Data handling (Graphs and interpretation)
- Week 8: 2D shapes (Properties and naming)
- Week 9: Revision
- Week 10: Assessment

## Term 2
- Week 1: Whole numbers (Rounding and mental math)
- Week 2: Addition and subtraction (6-digit numbers)
- Week 3: Common fractions (Adding and subtracting)
- Week 4: Length (Measurement and calculations)
- Week 5: Mass (Measurement and calculations)
- Week 6: Properties of 2D shapes (Symmetry)
- Week 7: Numeric patterns (Input/Output)
- Week 8: Whole numbers (Multiplication)
- Week 9: Revision
- Week 10: Assessment

## Term 3
- Week 1: Whole numbers (6-digit multiplication)
- Week 2: Whole numbers (6-digit division)
- Week 3: Common fractions (Diagrams and equivalent fractions)
- Week 4: Mass and Capacity (Revision)
- Week 5: Viewing objects (3D perspectives)
- Week 6: Properties of 3D objects
- Week 7: Data handling (Pictographs and Pie charts)
- Week 8: Geometric patterns
- Week 9: Revision
- Week 10: Assessment

## Term 4
- Week 1: Whole numbers (Mental math and estimation)
- Week 2: Number sentences (Inverse operations)
- Week 3: Common fractions (Problem solving)
- Week 4: Perimeter, Area, and Volume
- Week 5: Position and movement (Grid maps)
- Week 6: Probability (Scale of probability)
- Week 7: Data handling (Summarizing data)
- Week 8: Final Revision
- Week 9: Final Assessment
- Week 10: Final Feedback

## Curriculum Resources
- [CAPS Document: mathematics.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade5/mathematics.pdf)

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 5 English.
output:
# Grade 5 English Home Language (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: Listening & Speaking (Group discussion: News)
- Week 2: Reading (Short story), Writing (Character description)
- Week 3: Language Structures (Verbs: Continuous tense)
- Week 4: Listening & Speaking (Storytelling with props)
- Week 5: Reading (Poetry), Writing (Acrostic poem)
- Week 6: Language Structures (Adjectives and Adverbs)
- Week 7: Reading (Mythology), Writing (Myth summary)
- Week 8: Language Structures (Punctuation: Commas and Colons)
- Week 9: Revision
- Week 10: Assessment

## Term 2
- Week 1: Listening & Speaking (Instructional oral)
- Week 2: Reading (Newspaper article), Writing (News report)
- Week 3: Language Structures (Prepositions and Phrases)
- Week 4: Listening & Speaking (Debate basics)
- Week 5: Reading (Drama excerpt), Writing (Dialogue script)
- Week 6: Language Structures (Past and Future continuous)
- Week 7: Reading (Informational text), Writing (Mind map/Summary)
- Week 8: Language Structures (Conjunctions: Compound sentences)
- Week 9: Revision
- Week 10: Assessment

## Term 3
- Week 1: Listening & Speaking (Prepared speech)
- Week 2: Reading (Historical fiction), Writing (Diary entry)
- Week 3: Language Structures (Direct and Indirect speech)
- Week 4: Listening & Speaking (Book talk)
- Week 5: Reading (Technical text), Writing (How-to guide)
- Week 6: Language Structures (Active and Passive voice - intro)
- Week 7: Reading (Comic strips), Writing (Original comic)
- Week 8: Language Structures (Punctuation: Hyphens and Dashes)
- Week 9: Revision
- Week 10: Assessment

## Term 4
- Week 1: Listening & Speaking (Persuasive oral)
- Week 2: Reading (Biographies), Writing (Autobiography)
- Week 3: Language Structures (Subject-Verb agreement)
- Week 4: Listening & Speaking (Role play: Conflict resolution)
- Week 5: Reading (Advertisements), Writing (Campaign poster)
- Week 6: Language Structures (Revision of all tenses)
- Week 7: Final Revision (Exam preparation)
- Week 8: Final Examination
- Week 9: Feedback and Moderation
- Week 10: Year-end activities

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 5 Natural sciences.
output:
# Grade 5 Natural Sciences (CAPS)

## Term 1: Life and Living
- Week 1: Plants and animals on Earth
- Week 2: Animal skeletons
- Week 3: Skeletons as structures
- Week 4: Food chains
- Week 5: Life cycles of plants
- Week 6: Processes of life (Growth, reproduction)
- Week 7: Biodiversity and interdependence
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 2: Matter and Materials
- Week 1: Metals and non-metals
- Week 2: Properties of metals
- Week 3: Uses of metals
- Week 4: Processing materials (Raw to processed)
- Week 5: Strengthening materials
- Week 6: Combining materials
- Week 7: Traditional processing (History link)
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 3: Energy and Change
- Week 1: Stored energy in fuels
- Week 2: Burning fuels (Input/Output)
- Week 3: Safety with fire
- Week 4: Cells and batteries
- Week 5: Mains electricity
- Week 6: Safety with electricity
- Week 7: Elastic and compressed springs
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 4: Earth and Beyond
- Week 1: Planet Earth (Structure)
- Week 2: Surface of the Earth (Soil and rocks)
- Week 3: Sedimentary rocks
- Week 4: Fossils
- Week 5: The Sun, Earth and Moon (Movement)
- Week 6: Phases of the Moon
- Week 7: Gravity (The force that keeps us on Earth)
- Week 8: Final Assessment

## Curriculum Resources
- [CAPS Document: natural-sciences-and-technology.pdf](../../../caps/pdf/grade5/natural-sciences-and-technology.pdf)

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 3 Afrikaans.
output:
# Graad 3 Afrikaans Eerste Addisionele Taal (KABV)

## Kwartaal 1
- Week 1: Groete en bekendstelling (Greetings and introduction)
- Week 2: My familie en vriende (My family and friends)
- Week 3: Luister en praat (Listening and speaking)
- Week 4: Lees en kyk (Reading and looking)
- Week 5: Klanke (Phonics)
- Week 6: Skryf en ontwerp (Writing and design)
- Week 7: Taalstrukture (Nouns and verbs)
- Week 8: Assessering

## Kwartaal 2
- Week 1: My skool (My school)
- Week 2: Klanke en spelling
- Week 3: Lees van stories
- Week 4: Skryf van sinne
- Week 5: Diere en hul geluide
- Week 6: Seisoene en weer
- Week 7: Taalstrukture (Adjectives)
- Week 8: Assessering

## Kwartaal 3
- Week 1: Gesonde gewoontes (Healthy habits)
- Week 2: Kos en voeding
- Week 3: Lees van gedigte
- Week 4: Skryf van kort paragrawe
- Week 5: Vervoer (Transport)
- Week 6: Plekke in die gemeenskap
- Week 7: Taalstrukture (Past tense)
- Week 8: Assessering

## Kwartaal 4
- Week 1: Ons land Suid-Afrika
- Week 2: Vakansies en pret
- Week 3: Lees van koerantartikels
- Week 4: Skryf van uitnodigings
- Week 5: Veiligheid (Safety)
- Week 6: Mondelinge aanbiedings
- Week 7: Hersiening
- Week 8: Finale Assessering

## Curriculum Resources

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 3 Sepedi.
output:
# Grade 3 Sepedi First Additional Language (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: Madume le boitsebišo (Greetings and introductions)
- Week 2: Polelo ya ka gae (Home language vocabulary)
- Week 3: Go bala mantšu le dipolelo (Reading words and sentences)
- Week 4: Go ngwala (Writing simple sentences)
- Week 5: Sebopego sa polelo (Grammar: Maina)
- Week 6: Go theeletša le go bolela (Listening and speaking)
- Week 7: Mareo a mmele (Body parts)
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko (Assessment)

## Term 2
- Week 1: Polelo ya sekolo (School vocabulary)
- Week 2: Go bala dikanegelo (Reading stories)
- Week 3: Go ngwala (Writing paragraphs)
- Week 4: Sebopego sa polelo (Grammar: Mašito)
- Week 5: Diphoofolo (Animals)
- Week 6: Mehlare le maloba (Trees and flowers)
- Week 7: Go theeletša le go bolela (Songs)
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko (Assessment)

## Term 3
- Week 1: Dijo (Food)
- Week 2: Go bala (Reading informational texts)
- Week 3: Go ngwala (Writing letters)
- Week 4: Sebopego sa polelo (Grammar: Madiri)
- Week 5: Boemo bja leratadima (Weather)
- Week 6: Dipalangwa (Transport)
- Week 7: Dipapadi (Sports)
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko (Assessment)

## Term 4
- Week 1: Setšo le ditšo (Culture and traditions)
- Week 2: Go bala (Poetry)
- Week 3: Go ngwala (Creative writing)
- Week 4: Sebopego sa polelo (Revision)
- Week 5: Melao ya tsela (Road rules)
- Week 6: Go theeletša le go bolela (Presentations)
- Week 7: Boitokišetšo bja ditlhahlobo (Exam prep)
- Week 8: Tshekatsheko ya mafelelo (Final assessment)

## Curriculum Resources

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 3 Arts culture.
output:
# Grade 3 Arts & Culture (CAPS)

## Term 1: Visual Arts
- Week 1: Elements of art (Line)
- Week 2: Elements of art (Shape)
- Week 3: Colour theory (Primary colours)
- Week 4: Textures and patterns
- Week 5: Drawing from observation
- Week 6: Creating a collage
- Week 7: Clay modelling
- Week 8: Art Exhibition & Assessment

## Term 2: Music
- Week 1: Introduction to rhythm (Beat)
- Week 2: High and low sounds (Pitch)
- Week 3: Fast and slow (Tempo)
- Week 4: Musical instruments of SA
- Week 5: Singing in a group
- Week 6: Traditional songs
- Week 7: Composing simple rhythms
- Week 8: Music Performance & Assessment

## Term 3: Drama
- Week 1: Expressing feelings through mime
- Week 2: Characterisation (Voice)
- Week 3: Characterisation (Movement)
- Week 4: Role-play (Real-life situations)
- Week 5: Creating a short skit
- Week 6: Puppetry
- Week 7: Drama games
- Week 8: Drama Performance & Assessment

## Term 4: Dance
- Week 1: Basic body movements
- Week 2: Coordination and balance
- Week 3: Traditional SA dances
- Week 4: Modern dance steps
- Week 5: Choreographing a short dance
- Week 6: Dance and story
- Week 7: Final Rehearsals
- Week 8: Final Dance Showcase

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 3 Life skills.
output:
# Grade 3 Life Skills (CAPS)

## Term 1
- Week 1: About me (Personal strengths)
- Week 2: Feelings and emotions
- Week 3: Health and hygiene
- Week 4: Safety at home and school
- Week 5: Physical Education (Basic movements)
- Week 6: Creative Arts (Drawing and painting)
- Week 7: Environment (Planting)
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 2
- Week 1: Rights and responsibilities
- Week 2: Conflict resolution
- Week 3: Nutrition (Healthy eating)
- Week 4: Physical Education (Games and rules)
- Week 5: Creative Arts (Music and dance)
- Week 6: Religion and culture (Festivals)
- Week 7: Recycling and waste
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 3
- Week 1: Public safety (Road safety)
- Week 2: First Aid basics
- Week 3: Careers and work
- Week 4: Physical Education (Athletics)
- Week 5: Creative Arts (Drama and performance)
- Week 6: Space (The solar system)
- Week 7: Water safety
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 4
- Week 1: Pollution (Causes and effects)
- Week 2: Conserving electricity
- Week 3: Emotional well-being (Resilience)
- Week 4: Physical Education (Team sports)
- Week 5: Creative Arts (Mixed media)
- Week 6: Social justice (Inclusivity)
- Week 7: Final Revision
- Week 8: Final Assessment

instruction: Generate a CAPS-aligned teaching plan overview for Grade 3 Social sciences.
output:
# Grade 3 Social Sciences (CAPS)

## Term 1: Geography - Maps and Symbols
- Week 1: Introduction to maps
- Week 2: Symbols and keys
- Week 3: Compass directions (NSEW)
- Week 4: Grid references
- Week 5: Local area maps
- Week 6: Landmarks in our community
- Week 7: Drawing a simple map
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 2: History - My Family and Community
- Week 1: Finding out about the past
- Week 2: Family history and stories
- Week 3: Community history
- Week 4: Local leaders and heroes
- Week 5: Changes over time in our area
- Week 6: Important local buildings
- Week 7: Commemorative events
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 3: Geography - Environmental Resources
- Week 1: Natural resources (Water)
- Week 2: Natural resources (Soil)
- Week 3: Natural resources (Plants/Forests)
- Week 4: Using resources responsibly
- Week 5: Farming in South Africa
- Week 6: Mining in South Africa
- Week 7: Trading resources
- Week 8: Revision & Assessment

## Term 4: History - Famous People and Inventions
- Week 1: Famous South African leaders
- Week 2: Inventors and their inventions
- Week 3: How technology has changed lives
- Week 4: Ancient civilizations (brief intro)
- Week 5: Historical sites in SA
- Week 6: Cultural heritage
- Week 7: Final Revision
- Week 8: Final Assessment
